tasks.text: Optimize rayAABB_inv by replacing 6 fminf/fmaxf calls with sign-based selection
SIMD-vectorize rayAABB_inv with __m128 intrinsics: replace scalar x/y/z computations with packed SSE operations, use mask-based near/far selection, and horizontal reductions for final tmin/tmax
Implement SIMD rayAABB_inv using __m128 intrinsics: load AABB mins/maxs as __m128, compute tx0/tx1 etc. in parallel, use _mm_min_ps/_mm_max_ps for selection, horizontal reduce for final tmin/tmax
Vectorize rayTriangle using __m128 intrinsics: compute cross products and dot products in parallel for h, a, u, q, v, t
Vectorize rayTriangle using __m128 intrinsics: load vertices as __m128, compute e1/e2 via sub, cross products h/q via shuffles, dot product a via dp_ps, then compute u/v/t with early exits
Vectorize rayTriangle using __m128 intrinsics: load vertices as __m128, compute e1/e2 via sub, cross products h/q via shuffles, dot product a via dp_ps, then compute u/v/t with early exits
Vectorize rayTriangle using __m128 intrinsics: load vertices as __m128, compute e1/e2 via sub, cross products h/q via shuffles, dot product a via dp_ps, then compute u/v/t with early exits
Clean up and optimize rayTriangle: remove comment bloat, use _mm_dp_ps for dot products, reduce shuffle count by pre-shuffling cross product operands
Clean up and optimize rayTriangle: remove comment bloat, use _mm_dp_ps for dot products, reduce shuffle count

notes.text: Baseline: 1101 frames in 10.0s, avg 9.057ms/frame, median 8.976ms. Primary hotspots: RayTraceRowFunc (97.7%), IntersectBVH (44.6%), rayAABB_inv (30.4%), rayTriangle (13.6%). These three functions together account for ~88.6% of total time. rayAABB_inv is the single largest exclusive hotspot at 30.2%.

Analysis of rayAABB_inv: The function is called ~2x per BVH node (left and right children). It computes 6 multiplications, 6 subtractions, 6 fmin/fmax calls, and 2 comparisons. The main cost is the 6 fmin/fmax calls per invocation. Since invRd and bias are constant for all BVH traversals of a single ray, and the AABB mins/maxs are aligned to 16 bytes (float3 + int padding in BVHNode), we can potentially vectorize or use SIMD intrinsics. However, the biggest win might be in the caller: IntersectBVH already precomputes invDir and bias. The current rayAABB_inv does mn[0]*invRd.x - bias.x etc. We can try to use SIMD (__m128) to process all 3 axes in parallel, or unroll/inline more aggressively. Another observation: the function is static inline in the header, so it should be inlined. Let's check if the compiler is actually inlining it by looking at the assembly or trying a SIMD version.

Plan: Optimize rayAABB_inv by replacing 6 fminf/fmaxf calls with sign-based selection of near/far planes per axis. This reduces the function to 3 comparisons (cheap, well-predicted) + 1 fmaxf + 1 fminf. Mathematically identical to original.
Baseline: 1072 frames in 10.0s, avg 9.301ms/frame. Hotspots: rayAABB_inv (30.8%), rayTriangle (12.5%), IntersectBVH (14.8%). rayAABB_inv is the largest exclusive hotspot.

Sign-based rayAABB_inv caused regression (-0.2% avg, MSE=61.93). The compiler was likely already handling the fminf/fmaxf efficiently with conditional moves. The explicit branches may have caused mispredictions. Need to revert and try a different optimization.

New approach: manually SIMD-vectorize rayAABB_inv using __m128 intrinsics. Process all 3 axes in parallel. This should reduce 3 separate multiplies/adds/subtracts into single SSE operations, and the 6 fminf/fmaxf into parallel SSE min/max. Since BVHNode stores mn/mx as 12 bytes each with 4 bytes padding, we can load them as 128-bit values.
Current rayAABB_inv (object.h:163-170) is scalar with 6 fminf/fmaxf calls. Previous sign-based optimization caused regression (MSE=61.93, -0.2% speed). Now attempting SIMD __m128 vectorization.
SIMD rayAABB_inv: 1072→1101 frames (+2.7%), avg_ms 9.301→9.065 (+2.5%), p99_ms 12.306→10.668 (+13.3%). MSE=0.00, visually identical. HOTSPOT DEFEATED.

Task #1 (sign-based rayAABB_inv) is obsolete — superseded by successful SIMD vectorization. Tasks #2 and #3 are done. Next hotspot to investigate: rayTriangle (~13.6% exclusive) or IntersectBVH (~14.8% inclusive but lower exclusive after rayAABB_inv optimization).

Next hotspot: rayTriangle (14.4% exclusive). The function computes Möller–Trumbore intersection. Key observations: 1) It creates temporary float3 structs for e1, e2, h, s, q which involves multiple subtractions/adds. 2) The cross product computations (h and q) can be vectorized. 3) The determinant 'a' check is a branch that might be predictable but still costly. 4) Division by 'a' followed by multiplication by 'f' could potentially be optimized. 5) The barycentric coordinate checks (u, v) involve multiple multiplications and comparisons. Let me look at the caller context to understand how this function is invoked.

rayTriangle analysis: The function is the 3rd largest hotspot (14.4% exclusive). It implements Möller-Trumbore algorithm. Key costs: 1) Three cross products (h, q) and dot product (a). 2) Multiple float3 struct allocations on stack. 3) Early exits on u, v, t checks. The function is static but not inline - could benefit from inlining or manual unrolling. Since it's called from IntersectBVH which iterates over triangles, optimizing this will have significant impact. Potential optimizations: a) Use SIMD (__m128) for cross products and dot products. b) Reorder computations to fail fast on early exits. c) Use reciprocal multiplication instead of division where possible. d) Consider if the compiler is already optimizing well.

rayTriangle SIMD vectorization plan: The function computes Möller-Trumbore intersection. Key operations: e1=v1-v0, e2=v2-v0 (subs), h=rd x e2 (cross), a=e1·h (dot), s=ro-v0 (sub), u=f*(s·h) where f=1/a, q=s x e1 (cross), v=f*(rd·q), t=f*(e2·q). All can be done with __m128 SSE intrinsics. Since float3 is 16 bytes with w padding, we can load vertices directly as __m128. Cross products require shuffles. Dot products use _mm_dp_ps or multiply+shuffle+add. Early exits on u,v,t checks will need extractions after SIMD computation.

rayTriangle is the 3rd largest hotspot (14.4% exclusive). It implements Möller-Trumbore intersection. The function creates temporary float3 structs for e1, e2, h, s, q which involves multiple subtractions/adds. The cross product computations (h and q) can be vectorized using SIMD (__m128). The determinant 'a' check is a branch that might be predictable but still costly. Division by 'a' followed by multiplication by 'f' could potentially be optimized. The function is static but not inline - adding inline keyword may help. Since it's called from IntersectBVH which iterates over triangles, optimizing this will have significant impact.

rayTriangle SIMD vectorization plan: The function computes Möller-Trumbore intersection. Key operations: e1=v1-v0, e2=v2-v0 (subs), h=rd x e2 (cross), a=e1·h (dot), s=ro-v0 (sub), u=f*(s·h) where f=1/a, q=s x e1 (cross), v=f*(rd·q), t=f*(e2·q). All can be done with __m128 SSE intrinsics. Since float3 is 16 bytes with w padding, we can load vertices directly as __m128. Cross products require shuffles. Dot products use _mm_dp_ps or multiply+shuffle+add. Early exits on u,v,t checks will need extractions after SIMD computation.

Current rayTriangle (object/object.c:436-541) has an SSE implementation but it's bloated with comments and incorrect shuffles. The code does 4 scalar shuffles for rd, e2, e1, s components separately, then does per-component cross products using mul/sub. This is inefficient because: 1) Too many shuffles (4x for rd alone). 2) Cross products are done component-wise with separate muls instead of leveraging _mm_shuffle_ps efficiently. 3) The dot products use manual add chains instead of _mm_dp_ps. 4) There's a lot of dead comment code that inflates the function size, potentially hurting instruction cache.

rayTriangle (object/object.c:436-541) has a bloated SSE implementation with excessive comments and redundant shuffles. The function creates many scalar-wide shuffle variables (rd_x, rd_y, rd_z, e1_x, etc.) when it could use fewer shuffles or _mm_dp_ps for dot products. The current code is correct in logic but has ~100 lines of which ~60 are comments or redundant shuffles. This inflates the function size, potentially hurting instruction cache and making optimization harder.
